stories_covered: AI model access and competitive moats
harry_takes: The Google-Windsurf deal structure exemplifies how FTC concerns force creative M&A workarounds rather than traditional acquisitions
People significantly underestimate the stickiness of legacy brand distribution in early-stage developer tools markets
Benchmark's continued portfolio strength across AI companies (McCore, Heygen, Fireworks) proves partner departures matter far less than investors fear
The vibe coding market TAM could be 50-100x larger than pro developer tools because it targets non-developers with minimal training needs
harry_boldest_quote: People greatly over exaggerate the impact that this has on Benchmark.
predictions: Jason: Lovable and Replit will capture most value in vibe coding due to brand lock-in despite larger TAMs
Lemkin: Developer spend per employee will grow from 200 to 10000 dollars monthly over next 2-3 years
Panel: Direct listing would have solved little; mega IPO pops are natural intermittent market phenomena not pricing failures
disagreements: Mild debate over whether Windsurf founders could have recovered Claude access post-OpenAI deal collapse; panel aligned that deceleration from 100M to 82M ARR forced rapid exit timing.
harry_role: Harry moderates, asks clarifying questions, and synthesizes panel arguments rather than advocating strong positions. He defers to Jason and Rory's deeper expertise on venture/startup mechanics but challenges details and pushes for nuance.
notable: Extended unpacking of FTC-driven creative deal structures and their second-order legal/operational risks, with detailed explanation of why Cognition's simultaneous acquisition avoids clear FTC violations.
